title: Practice Test: Et tu, Brute? The real story of Brutus’ betrayal of Caesar / ‘Rescuing’ baby animals may amount to kidnapping / The Messier Marathon is coming. Here’s how to see 110 deep-sky wonders in one night.

passages.title: Et tu, Brute? The real story of Brutus’ betrayal of Caesar
passages.sourceTitle: Et tu, Brute? The real story of Brutus’ betrayal of Caesar
passages.contentHtml:
A Marcus Junius Brutus was a Roman politician, leader, orator—and one of history’s most infamous assassins. Despite Julius Caesar having spared his life, Brutus murdered him. In Caesar’s final moments, surrounded by conspirators, there was no more painful stab wound than that inflicted by Brutus.

B Brutus was born the son of a patrician and a wealthy plebeian. His mother, Servilia, maintained a long, romantic relationship with Caesar. Some historians speculate that Caesar might have fathered Brutus, though this is unlikely.

C Brutus later became a political supporter of Pompey against Caesar. He believed Pompey represented a more just option against tyranny. Brutus joined the faction of the optimates and took up arms against Caesar, his mother’s lover.

D When Caesar defeated Pompey, Brutus’s life seemed over. However, Caesar pardoned him, and Brutus enjoyed Caesar’s favor for four years, even becoming governor of Cisalpine Gaul.

E By 44 B.C., Brutus was a praetor, thanks to Caesar’s support. However, he was drawn into a conspiracy against Caesar by his brother-in-law, Gaius Cassius Longinus. Brutus objected to the dictatorship, but Cassius hated the ruler.

F The conspiracy succeeded in assassinating Caesar on March 15, 44 B.C., but failed to restore the Republic. The senators did not support the tyrannicide, and Brutus was unable to deliver his prepared speech.

G The conspirators, including Brutus, took refuge on Capitoline Hill. Cicero advised Brutus to convene the Senate to condemn Caesar as a tyrant, but Brutus did not follow this advice.

passages.title: ‘Rescuing’ baby animals may amount to kidnapping
passages.sourceTitle: ‘Rescuing’ baby animals may amount to kidnapping
passages.contentHtml:
A Each spring, wildlife rescuers face an influx of baby animals brought in by well-meaning people. These animals often appear lost but are usually waiting for their mothers.

B Connie Hall, who runs Magnolia Fawn Rescue, experienced this when a couple brought in a fawn they had taken from the wild. The fawn, deprived of its mother’s milk, died in her arms.

C Wildlife rehabilitators call this issue 'kidnap with intent to rescue.' Many animals, like deer and rabbits, are left alone by their mothers to protect them from predators.

D Baby animals like deer, rabbits, and songbirds are common victims. People often mistake these animals for orphans, not realizing the mothers are nearby.

E The problem is widespread, with many animals being taken unnecessarily. For example, a significant percentage of jackrabbits and cottontail rabbits brought to rehab centers are kidnapped.

F Kidnapped animals face numerous risks, including dehydration, stress, and improper feeding, which can lead to fatal conditions.

G In some regions, taking an animal can lead to euthanasia due to legal restrictions on rehabilitating certain species, such as deer in areas with chronic wasting disease.

passages.title: The Messier Marathon is coming. Here’s how to see 110 deep-sky wonders in one night.
passages.sourceTitle: The Messier Marathon is coming. Here’s how to see 110 deep-sky wonders in one night.
passages.contentHtml:
A Each spring, amateur astronomers take on the challenge of the Messier Marathon, attempting to spot all 110 objects in Charles Messier’s catalog in one night.

B The marathon is possible only during a short window in late March or early April, when all the objects can be seen from dusk till dawn.

C The Messier catalog includes famous objects like the Andromeda Galaxy and the Orion Nebula. Most can be seen with modest telescopes or binoculars.

D Completing the marathon requires careful planning and stamina, even with modern telescopes that can automatically locate celestial targets.

E The best time for the marathon is during a new moon, which makes faint objects easier to spot. Preparation is key, including choosing a good observation site.

F Observers must start immediately after sunset and continue throughout the night, adjusting for real-time conditions like cloud cover.

G The marathon is primarily a Northern Hemisphere event, and observers should plan the order of observations carefully to maximize their chances of success.
